title: You just got a new server
problem: You have been given SSH access to a Linux server you know nothing about. Find out: what OS is it running, how much RAM and disk it has, who else is logged in, what services are running, and how long it has been online. A real engineer does this before touching anything.

setup.description: No setup needed. Run these on your own Linux machine, WSL, or any VPS.

hints: Every answer is a single command — uname, uptime, free, df, who. Just try them.
Add flags to get more detail — try uname -a, free -h, df -h
For running services try systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running
solution:
1. Check OS info:
   uname -a
   # Prints kernel version, architecture, and OS name — always start here on an unknown server.
2. Get hostname:
   hostname
   # The server's network name — useful when managing multiple machines at once.
3. Check uptime:
   uptime
   # Shows how long the server has been running and the current load average.
4. Check RAM:
   free -h
   # Human-readable RAM and swap usage — the -h flag converts raw bytes to MB/GB.
5. Check disk:
   df -h
   # Disk usage per partition — spot full or nearly-full mounts before they cause problems.
6. See logged-in users:
   who
   # Lists everyone currently logged in with their terminal and login time.
7. List running services:
   systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running
   # Shows all active systemd services — note anything unexpected before you start work.
